content.problemStatement:
Given a string s containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid.

An input string is valid if:

Open brackets must be closed using the same type of brackets.
Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
Every close bracket has a corresponding open bracket of the same type.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a non‑empty string s consisting only of the characters '(' , ')' , '{' , '}' , '[' and ']'.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "true" if the string is a valid parentheses sequence, otherwise output "false".

sampleTestCases.explanation: First string is perfectly nested, second mixes three types correctly, third has a mismatched closing bracket.
First string is valid across all three bracket types, second violates the required order.
A single pair of parentheses is valid.

solution.approach: Use a stack to keep track of opening brackets. Scan the string from left to right. When an opening bracket is seen, push it onto the stack. When a closing bracket is seen, the stack must be non‑empty and its top must be the matching opening bracket; otherwise the string is invalid. Pop the matched opening bracket. After processing the whole string the stack must be empty for the string to be valid. This runs in linear time and uses linear extra space in the worst case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(|s|) per test case, O(total length of all strings) overall.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(|s|) auxiliary stack space in the worst case.
